text: Postoperative perineal hernia is a rare and difficult to treat complication of abdominal perineal excision. There are few records in literature and its repair remains an open issue, since there isn't a shared standard technique.

Postoperative perineal hernia is a rare and difficult to treat complication of abdominal perineal excision. There are few records in literature and its repair remains an open issue, since there isn't a shared standard technique.

Surgical repair can be achieved using different approaches. Transabdominal, transperineal, or combined abdominal perineal repair. The defect can be closed by primary suture, mesh placement, or by creating a flap with autologous tissue.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a postoperative perineal hernia, utilizing a Proceed mesh to reinforce the defect area.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a postoperative perineal hernia, utilizing a Proceed mesh to reinforce the defect.

A seventy-five-year-old woman that underwent abdominoperineal resection for a squamous cell carcinoma of the anus about thirty years before, presented with perineal bulging and discomfort. The pelvic MRI showed the uterus protruding through the pelvic floor into the perineal area.

A seventy-five-year-old woman that underwent abdominoperineal resection for a squamous cell carcinoma of the anus about thirty years before, presented with perineal bulging and discomfort. The pelvic MRI showed the uterus protruding through the pelvic floor into the perineal area.

During laparoscopic exploration, the surgeon observes few intra-abdominal adhesions. Two 10mm ports are positioned right paraumbilical and in the right lower abdomen, while a 5mm trocar is placed on the left mammary line at the umbilical level. The patient is in Trendelenburg position to facilitate the removal of small bowel and omentum from the pelvis.

During laparoscopic exploration, the surgeon observes few intra-abdominal adhesions. Two 10mm ports are positioned right paraumbilical and in the right lower abdomen, while a 5mm trocar is placed on the left mammary line at the umbilical level. The patient is in Trendelenburg position to facilitate the removal of small bowel and omentum from the pelvis.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap and identifies the peritoneal hernia located between the levator ani muscles. A laparoscopic primary closure of the hernia defect is performed using an absorbable running suture.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap and identifies the peritoneal hernia situated between the levator ani muscles. A laparoscopic primary closure of the hernia defect is performed using an absorbable running suture.

A previously shaped Proceed mesh is inserted into the abdomen through a ten-millimeter trocar. The mesh is positioned above the suture line and fixed laterally and posteriorly with absorbable tacks. Anteriorly, the mesh is secured to the posterior face of the vagina using surgical glue.

The surgeon performs a running suture of the peritoneum using a barbed suture, completing the repair of the perineal post-operative hernia.

The surgeon performs a running suture of the peritoneum using a barbed suture, completing the repair of the perineal post-operative hernia. The surgical field shows the peritoneum being closed with the suture material.
There were no intraoperative complications.

The patient started soft diet intake and ambulation on postoperative day two and was discharged on postoperative day four. She complained perineal pain during the two weeks following the procedure that was successfully treated with mild pain medications.

She complained perineal pain during the two weeks following the procedure that was successfully treated with mild pain medications. During the six months follow-up there was no recurrence of the perineal hernia.

During the six months follow-up there was no recurrence of the perineal hernia. We believe that this technique is feasible, safe and effective and could be a valid surgical option to repair post-operative perineal hernia.
